Just realizing this, it may make sense to hoist this to the CLI functional tests (depending on what direction we go as it relates to the file the cliArgumentRenames). Mainly if the plan is to put these in the `cli-extras.json`, botocore will not automatically load the up because botocore will only look for `sdk-extras.json` and therefore from it's standpoint, it will never see the `cliArgumentName`. If it were in the `cli-extras.json` (assuming we add support for that extension in the CLI), the CLI will be able to pick up the cliArgumentRenames in a CLI driver's session.

However if the values are going to be part of the `service-2.json` and not in the `extras.json`, this will not be a problem and the tests can live in botocore or the CLI. But if we put it in the CLI functional tests, it will not matter if the final resting place is the `cli-extra.json` or `service-2.json`. It will be loaded no matter what for the tests.

I recommend using `data = numpy.asarray(data)` here. I think this is the recommended way to go from a matrix to an array. This is *much* faster on large data.  }

Glad you asked :D I actually intend to filter more than just `elasticapm.`. In https://github.com/elastic/apm-agent-python/pull/164, I introduce a `functools.partial` call. In CPython, this doesn't create a frame (I suppose because it's implemented in C? Not sure). But in PyPy, this creates a frame, with the module `_functools`. So my intention is to rebase that PR after merging this one, and then adding `_functools` to that list. But we only want to kill that one frame at the top, and not any `functool.partial` calls that come later on.

Another argument for not filtering out _all_ frames that match is that this should only be used to cut away the top frames of the stack trace that are introduced by stack collection itself. If you call into any `elasticapm` code in other parts of the stack, that should IMO not be suppressed.

This code doesn't work with dynamic shapes. (In fact, it will hard-error if `edges.get_shape()` is `TensorShape(None)`.) We'd like all plugins to accept dynamic shapes whenever possible.\*

For the rank check: [`tf.assert_rank`].

For the shape check: you want `tf.shape(tensor)`, which gives you a tensor containing the shape data; then, you can use a `tf.assert_*` with control dependencies.

[`tf.assert_rank`]: https://www.tensorflow.org/versions/master/api_docs/python/tf/assert_rank

\* Here, "dynamic" means "not known to the TensorFlow compiler, which is kind of smart but not omniscient." Shapes can be lost in translation in various points of normal use. We have tests for this:  
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/dad4df940deaf5b94751c125aeea1cee56c67522/tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_test.py#L130-L136

May also be worth thinking forwards to the "multiple schemas" scenario (topic tree vs _____), in case that influences how we do things now.

For instance, imagine we're moving from Kolibri 0.19 to 0.20, and we add a new kind of channel, say "world map", which uses different models to represent the underlying metadata. Obviously channels of this type will require 0.20+, but we may at the same time still be publishing topic tree channels for the new version that are backwards compatible with 0.19 and below.

Or maybe, for backwards compatibility and for things like coach reports, we'll always have that topic tree in there, and the different channel types just add additional data structures that allow it to be navigated in a different way. But that may not be as adaptable to radically different types of content, such as game worlds or wiki sites.
